**[NSW](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales")**  **ACT**  **[WA](/wiki/Western_Australia "Western Australia")**  **[NT](/wiki/Northern_Territory "Northern Territory")**  **[SA](/wiki/South_Australia "South Australia")**  **[VIC](/wiki/Victoria_(state) "Victoria (state)")**  **[TAS](/wiki/Tasmania "Tasmania")** [States and territories of Australia](/wiki/File:Australian_Capital_Territory_in_Australia.svg "States and territories of Australia")  Location of ACT in Australia [35°18′29″S 149°07′28″E﻿ / ﻿35.30806°S 149.12444°E﻿ / -35.30806; 149.12444](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=Australian_Capital_Territory&params=35_18_29_S_149_07_28_E_) | |

(**ACT**), known as the **Federal Capital Territory** until 1938, is an internal [territory](/wiki/States_and_territories_of_Australia "States and territories of Australia") of Australia. [Canberra](/wiki/Canberra "Canberra"), the capital city of Australia, is situated within the territory, and is the territory's [primate city](/wiki/Primate_city "Primate city"). It is located in southeastern [Australian mainland](/wiki/Australian_mainland "Australian mainland") as an [enclave](/wiki/Enclave_and_exclave "Enclave and exclave") surrounded by the state of [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales") (NSW). Exclaved from NSW after [federation](/wiki/Federation_of_Australia "Federation of Australia") as the [seat of government](/wiki/Seat_of_government "Seat of government") for the new nation, the territory hosts [parliament house](/wiki/Parliament_House,_Canberra "Parliament House, Canberra"), [High Court of Australia](/wiki/High_Court_of_Australia "High Court of Australia") and the head offices of many [Australian Government](/wiki/Australian_Government "Australian Government") agencies.

The **Australian Capital Territory** (**ACT**), known as the **Federal Capital Territory** until 1938, is an internal [territory](/wiki/States_and_territories_of_Australia "States and territories of Australia") of Australia. [Canberra](/wiki/Canberra "Canberra"), the capital city of Australia, is situated within the territory, and is the territory's [primate city](/wiki/Primate_city "Primate city"). It is located in southeastern [Australian mainland](/wiki/Australian_mainland "Australian mainland") as an [enclave](/wiki/Enclave_and_exclave "Enclave and exclave") surrounded by the state of [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales") (NSW). Exclaved from NSW after [federation](/wiki/Federation_of_Australia "Federation of Australia") as the [seat of government](/wiki/Seat_of_government "Seat of government") for the new nation, the territory hosts [parliament house](/wiki/Parliament_House,_Canberra "Parliament House, Canberra"), [High Court of Australia](/wiki/High_Court_of_Australia "High Court of Australia") and the head offices of many [Australian Government](/wiki/Australian_Government "Australian Government") agencies.

On 1 January 1901, [federation of the colonies](/wiki/Federation_of_Australia "Federation of Australia") of Australia was achieved. Section 125 of the new [Australian Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Australia "Constitution of Australia") provided that land, situated in New South Wales and at least 100 miles (160 km) from [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney"), would be ceded to the new [federal government](/wiki/Government_of_Australia "Government of Australia"). Following discussion and exploration of various areas within New South Wales, the *[Seat of Government Act 1908](/wiki/Seat_of_Government_Act_1908 "Seat of Government Act 1908")* was passed in 1908 which specified a capital in the Yass-Canberra region. The territory was transferred to the federal government by [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales") in 1911, two years prior to the capital city being founded and formally named as Canberra in 1913.

While the overwhelming majority of the territory population resides in the city of [Canberra](/wiki/Canberra "Canberra") in the territory's north-east, the territory also includes some towns such as [Williamsdale](/wiki/Williamsdale,_Australian_Capital_Territory "Williamsdale, Australian Capital Territory"), [Oaks Estate](/wiki/Oaks_Estate,_Australian_Capital_Territory "Oaks Estate, Australian Capital Territory"), [Uriarra](/wiki/Uriarra,_Australian_Capital_Territory "Uriarra, Australian Capital Territory"), [Tharwa](/wiki/Tharwa,_Australian_Capital_Territory "Tharwa, Australian Capital Territory") and [Hall](/wiki/Hall,_Australian_Capital_Territory "Hall, Australian Capital Territory"). The territory also includes the [Namadgi National Park](/wiki/Namadgi_National_Park "Namadgi National Park"), which comprises the majority of land area of the territory. Despite a common misconception, the [Jervis Bay Territory](/wiki/Jervis_Bay_Territory "Jervis Bay Territory") is not part of the ACT, although ACT laws apply in the Jervis Bay Territory.[[20]](#cite_note-:1-23) The territory has a relatively dry, continental climate, experiencing warm to hot summers and cool to cold winters. Among the [states and territories of Australia](/wiki/States_and_territories_of_Australia "States and territories of Australia"), the Australian Capital Territory is the only one which is [landlocked](/wiki/Landlocked "Landlocked").

The territory is home to many important institutions of the federal government, national monuments and museums. These include the [Parliament of Australia](/wiki/Parliament_of_Australia "Parliament of Australia"), the [High Court of Australia](/wiki/High_Court_of_Australia "High Court of Australia"), the [National Gallery of Australia](/wiki/National_Gallery_of_Australia "National Gallery of Australia"), the [Australian Defence Force Academy](/wiki/Australian_Defence_Force_Academy "Australian Defence Force Academy") and the [Australian War Memorial](/wiki/Australian_War_Memorial "Australian War Memorial"). It also hosts the majority of foreign embassies in Australia, as well as regional headquarters of many international organisations, not-for-profit groups, lobbying groups and professional associations. Several major universities also have campuses in the territory, including the [Australian National University](/wiki/Australian_National_University "Australian National University"), the [University of Canberra](/wiki/University_of_Canberra "University of Canberra"), the [University of New South Wales](/wiki/University_of_New_South_Wales "University of New South Wales"), [Charles Sturt University](/wiki/Charles_Sturt_University "Charles Sturt University") and the [Australian Catholic University](/wiki/Australian_Catholic_University "Australian Catholic University").

[A locally elected legislative assembly](/wiki/Australian_Capital_Territory_Legislative_Assembly "Australian Capital Territory Legislative Assembly") has governed the territory since 1988. However, the Commonwealth maintains authority over the territory and may disallow or overturn local laws. The Commonwealth maintains control over the area known as the [Parliamentary Triangle](/wiki/Parliamentary_Triangle,_Canberra "Parliamentary Triangle, Canberra") through the [National Capital Authority](/wiki/National_Capital_Authority "National Capital Authority"). Residents of the territory, together with residents of the [Jervis Bay Territory](/wiki/Jervis_Bay_Territory "Jervis Bay Territory") and [Norfolk Island](/wiki/Norfolk_Island "Norfolk Island"), elect three members of the [House of Representatives](/wiki/Australian_House_of_Representatives "Australian House of Representatives") and two [senators](/wiki/Australian_Senate "Australian Senate"). While senators who represent Australian states have fixed six-year terms, the terms of Territory senators are not fixed but are governed by the opening and dissolution of the House of Representatives.

With 475,600 residents, the territory is the second smallest mainland state or territory by population. At the [2016 census](/wiki/2016_Australian_census "2016 Australian census"), the median weekly income for people in the territory aged over 15 was $998, significantly higher than the national median of $662.[[21]](#cite_note-census-24) The average level of degree qualification in the territory is also higher than the national average. Within the territory, 37.1% of the population hold a bachelor's degree level or above education compared to the national figure of 20%.[[21]](#cite_note-census-24) With high standards of living; in 2023, the territory recorded the second highest subnational [Human Development Index](/wiki/Human_Development_Index "Human Development Index") (HDI) score of 0.988 [out of over 1,700](/wiki/List_of_subnational_entities_with_the_highest_and_lowest_Human_Development_Index "List of subnational entities with the highest and lowest Human Development Index") in the world, just after [Zurich](/wiki/Zurich "Zurich").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Australian_Capital_Territory&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of the Australian Capital Territory](/wiki/History_of_the_Australian_Capital_Territory "History of the Australian Capital Territory")

### Indigenous inhabitants

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Australian_Capital_Territory&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Indigenous inhabitants")]

[Indigenous Australians](/wiki/Aboriginal_Australians "Aboriginal Australians") have long inhabited the area.[[22]](#cite_note-:2-25) Evidence indicates habitation dating back at least 25,000 years,[[23]](#cite_note-26) and it is possible that the area was inhabited for considerably longer. The principal group occupying the region were the [Ngunnawal people](/wiki/Ngunnawal "Ngunnawal"), with the [Ngarigo](/wiki/Ngarigo "Ngarigo") and [Walgalu](/wiki/Walgalu "Walgalu") living immediately to the south, the [Wandadian](/wiki/Wandandian "Wandandian") to the east, the [Gandangara](/wiki/Gandangara "Gandangara") to the north and the [Wiradjuri](/wiki/Wiradjuri "Wiradjuri") to the north-west.[[24]](#cite_note-Gillespie84-27)

### European colonisation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Australian_Capital_Territory&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: European colonisation")]

Following European settlement, the growth of the new colony of New South Wales led to an increasing demand for [arable land](/wiki/Arable_land "Arable land").[[24]](#cite_note-Gillespie84-27) Governor [Lachlan Macquarie](/wiki/Lachlan_Macquarie "Lachlan Macquarie") supported expeditions to open up new lands to the south of [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney").[[25]](#cite_note-:3-28) The 1820s saw further exploration in the Canberra area associated with the construction of a road from Sydney to the [Goulburn](/wiki/Goulburn "Goulburn") plains. While working on the project, [Charles Throsby](/wiki/Charles_Throsby "Charles Throsby") learned of a nearby lake and river from the local Indigenous peoples and he accordingly sent Wild to lead a small party to investigate the site. The search was unsuccessful, but they did discover the [Yass River](/wiki/Yass_River "Yass River"), and it is surmised that they would have set foot on part of the future territory.[[26]](#cite_note-:4-29)

A second expedition was mounted shortly thereafter, and they became the first Europeans to camp at the [Molonglo](/wiki/Molonglo_River "Molonglo River") (Ngambri) and [Queanbeyan](/wiki/Queanbeyan_River "Queanbeyan River") (Jullergung) Rivers.[[22]](#cite_note-:2-25) However, they failed to find the [Murrumbidgee River](/wiki/Murrumbidgee_River "Murrumbidgee River").[[26]](#cite_note-:4-29) The issue of the Murrumbidgee was solved in 1821 when Throsby mounted a third expedition and successfully reached the watercourse, on the way providing the first detailed account of the land that now comprises the territory.[[26]](#cite_note-:4-29) The last expedition in the region before settlement was undertaken by [Allan Cunningham](/wiki/Allan_Cunningham_(botanist) "Allan Cunningham (botanist)") in 1824.[[22]](#cite_note-:2-25) He reported that the region was suitable for grazing and the settlement of the Limestone Plains followed immediately thereafter.[[27]](#cite_note-30)

### Early settlement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Australian_Capital_Territory&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Early settlement")]

[![Map locations concentrated in the northeast and east, west and south largely deserted except for the southern tip](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/ACT_historic_buildings_map-MJC2.png/250px-ACT_historic_buildings_map-MJC2.png)](/wiki/File:ACT_historic_buildings_map-MJC2.png)

Significant homesteads, structures and settlements in the ACT prior to 1909.

The first land grant in the region was made to Joshua John Moore in 1823, and European settlement in the area began in 1824 with the construction of a homestead by his stockmen on what is now the [Acton Peninsula](/wiki/Acton_Peninsula "Acton Peninsula").[[25]](#cite_note-:3-28) Moore formally purchased the site in 1826 and named the property *Canberry* or *Canberra*.[[22]](#cite_note-:2-25)

A significant influx of population and economic activity occurred around the 1850s [gold rushes](/wiki/Australian_gold_rushes "Australian gold rushes").[[28]](#cite_note-31) The gold rushes prompted the establishment of communication between Sydney and the region by way of the [Cobb & Co](/wiki/Cobb_and_Co "Cobb and Co") coaches, which transported mail and passengers.[[29]](#cite_note-:5-32) The first post offices opened in [Ginninderra](/wiki/Ginninderra "Ginninderra") in 1859 and at Lanyon in 1860.[[25]](#cite_note-:3-28)

During colonial times, the European communities of Ginninderra, [Molonglo](/wiki/Molonglo_Plains "Molonglo Plains") and Tuggeranong settled and farmed the surrounding land. The region was also called the [Queanbeyan](/wiki/Queanbeyan "Queanbeyan")-[Yass](/wiki/Yass,_New_South_Wales "Yass, New South Wales") district, after the two largest towns in the area. The villages of Ginninderra and Tharwa developed to service the local agrarian communities.

During the first 20 years of settlement, there was only limited contact between the settlers and Aboriginal people. Over the succeeding years, the Ngunnawal and other local indigenous people effectively ceased to exist as cohesive and independent communities adhering to their traditional ways of life.[[25]](#cite_note-:3-28) Those who had not succumbed to disease and other predations either dispersed to the local settlements or were relocated to more distant [Aboriginal reserves](/wiki/Aboriginal_reserve "Aboriginal reserve") set up by the New South Wales government in the latter part of the 19th century.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Creation of the territory

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Australian_Capital_Territory&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Creation of the territory")]

[![Single wooden cabin and many white tents in open dusty field with single tree in foreground](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/Surveyors_camp_Canberra.jpg/250px-Surveyors_camp_Canberra.jpg)](/wiki/File:Surveyors_camp_Canberra.jpg)

The Federal Capital survey camp was established c. 1909. An extensive survey of the territory was completed by Charles Scrivener and his team in 1915.

In 1898, a referendum on a proposed Constitution was held in four of the colonies – [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales"), [South Australia](/wiki/South_Australia "South Australia"), [Tasmania](/wiki/Tasmania "Tasmania"), and [Victoria](/wiki/Victoria_(Australia) "Victoria (Australia)"). Although the referendum achieved a majority in all four colonies, the New South Wales referendum failed to gain the minimum number of votes needed for the bill to pass. Following this result, a meeting of the four Premiers in 1898 heard from [George Reid](/wiki/George_Reid "George Reid"), the [Premier of New South Wales](/wiki/Premier_of_New_South_Wales "Premier of New South Wales"), who argued that locating the future capital in New South Wales would be sufficient to ensure the passage of the Bill. The 1899 referendum on this revised bill was successful and passed with sufficient numbers.[[30]](#cite_note-33) Section 125 of the [Australian Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Australia "Constitution of Australia") thus provided that, following Federation in 1901, land would be ceded freely to the new [federal government](/wiki/Australian_Government "Australian Government").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

This, however, left open the question of where to locate the capital. In 1906 and after significant deliberations, New South Wales agreed to cede sufficient land on the condition that it was in the [Yass](/wiki/Yass,_New_South_Wales "Yass, New South Wales")-[Canberra](/wiki/Canberra "Canberra") region,[[22]](#cite_note-:2-25) this site being closer to Sydney.[[31]](#cite_note-ausmap-34) Initially, [Dalgety, New South Wales](/wiki/Dalgety,_New_South_Wales "Dalgety, New South Wales") remained at the forefront, but Yass-Canberra prevailed after voting by federal representatives.[[26]](#cite_note-:4-29) The *[Seat of Government Act 1908](/wiki/Seat_of_Government_Act_1908 "Seat of Government Act 1908")* was passed in 1908, which repealed the 1904 Act and specified a capital in the Yass-Canberra region.[[22]](#cite_note-:2-25)[[32]](#cite_note-35) Government surveyor [Charles Scrivener](/wiki/Charles_Scrivener "Charles Scrivener") was deployed to the region in the same year to map out a specific site and, after an extensive search, settled upon the present location,[[33]](#cite_note-36) basing the borders primarily on the need to secure a stable water supply for the planned capital.[[34]](#cite_note-37)

The territory was transferred to the Commonwealth by [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales") on 1 January 1911, two years before the naming of Canberra as the national capital on 20 March 1913.[[36]](#cite_note-39)

The Commonwealth gained control of all land within the borders of the new territory but ownership only of NSW Crown land, with significant parcels of extant freehold remaining in the hands of their pre-existing owners. Much of this was acquired during [World War One](/wiki/World_War_One "World War One"), though a few titles were not transferred until the late 20th Century.

Land within the territory is granted under a [leasehold](/wiki/Leasehold "Leasehold") system, with [99-year residential leases](/wiki/99-year_lease "99-year lease") sold to buyers as new suburbs are planned, surveyed, and developed. The current policy is for these leases to be extended for another 99-year period on expiry, subject to payment of an administrative fee.[[37]](#cite_note-40) In an arrangement inspired by [Georgism](/wiki/Georgism "Georgism"), the ideas of 19th-century American economist [Henry George](/wiki/Henry_George "Henry George"), leaseholders had to pay 5% of the unimproved value of the underlying land in rent until the [Gorton government](/wiki/Gorton_government "Gorton government") abolished it in 1970.[[38]](#cite_note-41)

### Development throughout 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Australian_Capital_Territory&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Development throughout 20th century")]

[![Five people on stone platform, from left: Man with mustache in military dress uniform and bushy tall black hat with chinstrap; man in military uniform with many medals and old fashioned naval officer's hat; man with white hair in suit with long dark coat and white collar; woman in Victorian dress with white hat; man with beard and wild hair in suit. Crowd and flags in background.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/Naming_of_city_of_canberra_capital_hill_1913.jpg/250px-Naming_of_city_of_canberra_capital_hill_1913.jpg)](/wiki/File:Naming_of_city_of_canberra_capital_hill_1913.jpg)

The ceremony for the naming of Canberra, 12 March 1913. Prime Minister [Andrew Fisher](/wiki/Andrew_Fisher "Andrew Fisher") is standing, centre, in dark suit. To his right is the Governor-General, [Lord Denman](/wiki/Thomas_Denman,_3rd_Baron_Denman "Thomas Denman, 3rd Baron Denman"), and to his left, [Lady Denman](/wiki/Gertrude_Denman,_Baroness_Denman "Gertrude Denman, Baroness Denman").

In 1911, an international competition to design the future capital was held; it was won by the Chicago architect [Walter Burley Griffin](/wiki/Walter_Burley_Griffin "Walter Burley Griffin") in 1912.[[25]](#cite_note-:3-28) The official naming of Canberra occurred on 12 March 1913 and construction began immediately.[[25]](#cite_note-:3-28)

After Griffin's departure following difficulty in implementing his project,[[39]](#cite_note-act-42) the [Federal Capital Advisory Committee](/wiki/Federal_Capital_Advisory_Committee "Federal Capital Advisory Committee") was established in 1920 to advise the government of the construction efforts.[[22]](#cite_note-:2-25) The committee had limited success meeting its goals. However, the chairman, [John Sulman](/wiki/John_Sulman "John Sulman"), was instrumental in applying the ideas of the [garden city movement](/wiki/Garden_city_movement "Garden city movement") to Griffin's plan. The committee was replaced in 1925 by the [Federal Capital Commission](/wiki/Federal_Capital_Commission "Federal Capital Commission").[[22]](#cite_note-:2-25)

In 1930, the ACT Advisory Council was established to advise the minister for territories on the community's concerns. In 1934, the [Supreme Court of the Australian Capital Territory](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_Australian_Capital_Territory "Supreme Court of the Australian Capital Territory") was established.[[40]](#cite_note-43)

From 1938 to 1957, the [National Capital Planning and Development Committee](/wiki/National_Capital_Planning_and_Development_Committee "National Capital Planning and Development Committee") continued to plan the further expansion of Canberra. However, it did not have executive power, and decisions were made on the development of Canberra without consulting the committee.[[29]](#cite_note-:5-32) During this time, Prime Minister [Robert Menzies](/wiki/Robert_Menzies "Robert Menzies") regarded the state of the national capital as an embarrassment.[[41]](#cite_note-:6-44)

After World War II, there was a shortage of housing and office space in Canberra.[[42]](#cite_note-45) A Senate Select Committee hearing was held in 1954 to address its development requirements. This Committee recommended the creation of a single planning body with executive power. Consequently, the [National Capital Planning and Development Committee](/wiki/National_Capital_Planning_and_Development_Committee "National Capital Planning and Development Committee") was replaced by the [National Capital Development Commission](/wiki/National_Capital_Development_Commission "National Capital Development Commission") in 1957.[[43]](#cite_note-46) The [National Capital Development Commission](/wiki/National_Capital_Development_Commission "National Capital Development Commission") ended four decades of disputes over the shape and design of [Lake Burley Griffin](/wiki/Lake_Burley_Griffin "Lake Burley Griffin") and construction was completed in 1964 after four years of work. The completion of the centrepiece of Griffin's design finally laid the platform for the development of Griffin's [Parliamentary Triangle](/wiki/Parliamentary_Triangle,_Canberra "Parliamentary Triangle, Canberra").[[41]](#cite_note-:6-44)

### Self-government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Australian_Capital_Territory&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Self-government")]

In 1978, an [advisory referendum was held](/wiki/1978_Australian_Capital_Territory_self-government_referendum "1978 Australian Capital Territory self-government referendum") to determine the views of citizens resident in the territory about whether there should be self-government. Just under 64 percent of voters rejected devolved government options, in favour of the status quo.[[44]](#cite_note-47) Nevertheless, in 1988, the new federal minister for the territory [Gary Punch](/wiki/Gary_Punch "Gary Punch") received a report recommending the abolition of the [National Capital Development Commission](/wiki/National_Capital_Development_Commission "National Capital Development Commission") and the formation of a locally elected government. Punch recommended that the [Hawke government](/wiki/Bob_Hawke "Bob Hawke") accept the report's recommendations and subsequently [Clyde Holding](/wiki/Clyde_Holding "Clyde Holding") introduced legislation to grant self-government to the territory in October 1988.[[45]](#cite_note-48)

The enactment on 6 December 1988 of the *[Australian Capital Territory (Self-Government) Act 1988](/wiki/Australian_Capital_Territory_(Self-Government)_Act_1988 "Australian Capital Territory (Self-Government) Act 1988")* established the framework for self-government.[[46]](#cite_note-ACT1988-49) The [first election](/wiki/1989_Australian_Capital_Territory_election "1989 Australian Capital Territory election") for the 17-member [Australian Capital Territory Legislative Assembly](/wiki/Australian_Capital_Territory_Legislative_Assembly "Australian Capital Territory Legislative Assembly") was held on 4 March 1989.[[47]](#cite_note-50)

The initial years of self-government were difficult and unstable.[[48]](#cite_note-anniv-51) A majority of territory residents had opposed self-government and had it imposed upon them by the federal parliament. At the first election, 4 of the 17 seats were won by anti-self-government single-issue parties due to a protest vote by disgruntled Canberrans and a total of 8 were won by minor parties and independents.[[48]](#cite_note-anniv-51)

In 1992, Labor won eight seats and the minor parties and independents won only three. Stability increased, and in 1995, [Kate Carnell](/wiki/Kate_Carnell "Kate Carnell") became the first elected Liberal chief minister. In 1998, Carnell became the first chief minister to be re-elected.

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Australian_Capital_Territory&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/ACT-Jervis_Bay-Sydney-MJC.png/250px-ACT-Jervis_Bay-Sydney-MJC.png)](/wiki/File:ACT-Jervis_Bay-Sydney-MJC.png)

Location of the ACT and Jervis Bay

The territory is 2,358 km2 (910 mi2) in area, slightly smaller than Luxembourg.

It is bounded by the [Bombala railway line](/wiki/Bombala_railway_line "Bombala railway line") in the east, the [watershed](/wiki/Drainage_divide "Drainage divide") of [Naas Creek](/wiki/Naas_(valley) "Naas (valley)") in the south, the watershed of the [Cotter River](/wiki/Cotter_River "Cotter River") in the west and the watershed of the [Molonglo River](/wiki/Molonglo_River "Molonglo River") in the north-east. These boundaries were set to give the territory an adequate water supply.[[49]](#cite_note-52) The territory extends about 88.5 km (55.0 mi) north-south between 35.124°S and 35.921°S, and 57.75 km (35.88 mi) west-east between 148.763°E and 149.399°E.[[note 1]](#cite_note-53) The city area of [Canberra](/wiki/Canberra "Canberra") occupies the north-eastern corner of this area.

The territory includes the city of [Canberra](/wiki/Canberra "Canberra") and some towns such as [Williamsdale](/wiki/Williamsdale,_Australian_Capital_Territory "Williamsdale, Australian Capital Territory"), [Oaks Estate](/wiki/Oaks_Estate "Oaks Estate"), [Uriarra Village](/wiki/Uriarra_Village "Uriarra Village"), [Tharwa](/wiki/Tharwa "Tharwa") and [Hall](/wiki/Hall,_Australian_Capital_Territory "Hall, Australian Capital Territory"). The territory also contains agricultural land ([sheep](/wiki/Domestic_sheep "Domestic sheep"), [dairy cattle](/wiki/Dairy_cattle "Dairy cattle"), [vineyards](/wiki/Vineyard "Vineyard") and small amounts of crops) and a large area of national park ([Namadgi National Park](/wiki/Namadgi_National_Park "Namadgi National Park")), much of it mountainous and forested.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/Swans_on_molonglo_river.jpg/250px-Swans_on_molonglo_river.jpg)](/wiki/File:Swans_on_molonglo_river.jpg)

The [Molonglo River](/wiki/Molonglo_River "Molonglo River"), located in the north-east of the region

Tidbinbilla is a locality to the south-west of Canberra that features the [Tidbinbilla Nature Reserve](/wiki/Tidbinbilla_Nature_Reserve "Tidbinbilla Nature Reserve") and the [Canberra Deep Space Communication Complex](/wiki/Canberra_Deep_Space_Communication_Complex "Canberra Deep Space Communication Complex"), operated by the United States' [NASA](/wiki/NASA "NASA") as part of its [Deep Space Network](/wiki/Deep_Space_Network "Deep Space Network"). The [Southern Tablelands Temperate Grassland](/wiki/Southern_Tablelands_Temperate_Grassland "Southern Tablelands Temperate Grassland") straddles the state.

The territory includes a large range of mountains, rivers and creeks, largely contained within the [Namadgi National Park](/wiki/Namadgi_National_Park "Namadgi National Park"). These include the Naas and Murrumbidgee Rivers.

In September 2022, a proposal was announced to change the border between NSW and the territory, the first change since the territory was created in 1911.[[50]](#cite_note-54) ACT chief minister [Andrew Barr](/wiki/Andrew_Barr "Andrew Barr") said NSW premier [Dominic Perrottet](/wiki/Dominic_Perrottet "Dominic Perrottet") had agreed to a proposed border change of 330 ha (1.3 sq mi) in the [Ginninderra](/wiki/Ginninderra "Ginninderra") watershed.[[51]](#cite_note-55)

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Australian_Capital_Territory&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Climate")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/Autumn_in_Canberra.jpg/250px-Autumn_in_Canberra.jpg)](/wiki/File:Autumn_in_Canberra.jpg)

[Autumn foliage](/wiki/Autumn_foliage "Autumn foliage") in [Canberra](/wiki/Canberra "Canberra")

The territory has a relatively dry, continental climate, experiencing warm to hot summers and cool to cold winters.[[52]](#cite_note-:0-56) Under the [Köppen-Geiger classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen-Geiger_climate_classification_system "Köppen-Geiger climate classification system"), the territory has an [oceanic climate](/wiki/Oceanic_climate "Oceanic climate") (*Cfb*).[[53]](#cite_note-57)

January is the hottest month with an average high of 27.7 °C.[[52]](#cite_note-:0-56) July is the coldest month when the average high drops to 11.2 °C (52.2 °F).[[52]](#cite_note-:0-56) The highest maximum temperature recorded in the territory was 44.0 °C on 4 January 2020. The lowest minimum temperature was −10.0 °C on 11 July 1971.[[52]](#cite_note-:0-56)

Rainfall varies significantly across the territory.[[52]](#cite_note-:0-56) Much higher rainfall occurs in the mountains to the west of Canberra compared to the east.[[52]](#cite_note-:0-56) The mountains act as a barrier during winter with the city receiving less rainfall.[[52]](#cite_note-:0-56) Average annual rainfall in the territory is 629mm and there is an average of 108 rain days annually.[[52]](#cite_note-:0-56) The wettest month is October, with an average rainfall of 65.3mm, and the driest month is June, with an average of 39.6mm.[[52]](#cite_note-:0-56)

Frost is common in the winter months. [Snow](/wiki/Snow_in_Australia "Snow in Australia") is rare in Canberra's city centre, but the surrounding areas get annual snowfall through winter and often the snow-capped mountains can be seen from the city. The last significant snowfall in the city centre was in 1968.[[52]](#cite_note-:0-56)

Smoke haze became synonymous with the 2019/2020 Australian summer. On 1 January 2020 Canberra had the worst air quality of any major city in the world, with an AQI of 7700 (USAQI 949).[[54]](#cite_note-58)

### Geology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Australian_Capital_Territory&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Geology")]

Main article: [Geology of the Australian Capital Territory](/wiki/Geology_of_the_Australian_Capital_Territory "Geology of the Australian Capital Territory")

Notable geological formations in the territory include the *Canberra Formation*, the *Pittman Formation*, *Black Mountain Sandstone* and *State Circle Shale*.

In the 1840s [fossils](/wiki/Fossil "Fossil") of [brachiopods](/wiki/Brachiopod "Brachiopod") and [trilobites](/wiki/Trilobite "Trilobite") from the [Silurian](/wiki/Silurian "Silurian") period were discovered at Woolshed Creek near [Duntroon](/wiki/Duntroon,_Australian_Capital_Territory "Duntroon, Australian Capital Territory"). At the time, these were the oldest fossils discovered in Australia, though this record has now been far surpassed.[[57]](#cite_note-61) Other specific geological places of interest include the State Circle cutting and the Deakin [anticline](/wiki/Anticline "Anticline").[[58]](#cite_note-62)[[59]](#cite_note-63)

The oldest rocks in the territory date from the [Ordovician](/wiki/Ordovician "Ordovician") around 480 million years ago. During this period the region along with most of Eastern Australia was part of the ocean floor; formations from this period include the *Black Mountain Sandstone* formation and the *Pittman Formation* consisting largely of [quartz](/wiki/Quartz "Quartz")-rich [sandstone](/wiki/Sandstone "Sandstone"), [siltstone](/wiki/Siltstone "Siltstone") and [shale](/wiki/Shale "Shale"). These formations became exposed when the ocean floor was raised by a major [volcanic activity](/wiki/Orogeny "Orogeny") in the [Devonian](/wiki/Devonian "Devonian") forming much of the east coast of Australia.

### Flora and fauna

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Australian_Capital_Territory&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Flora and fauna")]

Main articles: [Fauna of the Australian Capital Territory](/wiki/Fauna_of_the_Australian_Capital_Territory "Fauna of the Australian Capital Territory") and [Flora of the Australian Capital Territory](/wiki/Flora_of_the_Australian_Capital_Territory "Flora of the Australian Capital Territory")

The environments range from [alpine](/wiki/Alpine_climate "Alpine climate") area on the higher mountains, to [sclerophyll](/wiki/Sclerophyll "Sclerophyll") forest and to [woodland](/wiki/Woodland "Woodland"). Much of the territory has been cleared for grazing and is also burnt off by [bushfires](/wiki/Bushfires_in_Australia "Bushfires in Australia") several times per century. The kinds of plants can be grouped into [vascular plants](/wiki/Vascular_plants "Vascular plants"), that include [gymnosperms](/wiki/Gymnosperm "Gymnosperm"), flowering plants, and [ferns](/wiki/Fern "Fern"), as well as [bryophytes](/wiki/Bryophyte "Bryophyte"), [lichens](/wiki/Lichen "Lichen"), fungi and freshwater [algae](/wiki/Algae "Algae"). Four flowering plants are endemic to the territory. Several lichens are unique to the territory. Most plants in the territory are characteristic of the [Flora of Australia](/wiki/Australian_flora "Australian flora") and include well known plants such as [Grevillea](/wiki/Grevillea "Grevillea"), [Eucalyptus](/wiki/Eucalyptus "Eucalyptus") trees and [kangaroo grass](/wiki/Kangaroo_grass "Kangaroo grass").

The native forest in the territory was almost wholly [eucalypt](/wiki/Eucalyptus "Eucalyptus") species and provided a resource for fuel and domestic purposes. By the early 1960s, logging had depleted the eucalypt, and concern about water quality led to the forests being closed. Interest in forestry began in 1915 with trials of a number of species including *[Pinus radiata](/wiki/Pinus_radiata "Pinus radiata")* on the slopes of Mount Stromlo. Since then, plantations have been expanded, with the benefit of reducing erosion in the Cotter catchment, and the forests are also popular recreation areas.[[60]](#cite_note-64)

Fauna in the territory includes representatives from most major [Australian animal groups](/wiki/Fauna_of_Australia "Fauna of Australia"). This includes kangaroos, wallabies, koalas, platypus, echidna, emu, kookaburras and dragon lizards.

### Territory government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Australian_Capital_Territory&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Territory government")]

See also: [Australian Capital Territory Legislative Assembly](/wiki/Australian_Capital_Territory_Legislative_Assembly "Australian Capital Territory Legislative Assembly"), [Electoral systems of the Australian states and territories](/wiki/Electoral_systems_of_the_Australian_states_and_territories "Electoral systems of the Australian states and territories"), and [Government of the Australian Capital Territory](/wiki/Government_of_the_Australian_Capital_Territory "Government of the Australian Capital Territory")

Unlike the states of Australia which have their own constitutions, territories are governed under a Commonwealth statute[[61]](#cite_note-:10-65)—for the ACT, the *[Australian Capital Territory (Self-Government) Act 1988](/wiki/Australian_Capital_Territory_(Self-Government)_Act_1988 "Australian Capital Territory (Self-Government) Act 1988")*.[[62]](#cite_note-66) The *Self-Government Act* constitutes a democratic government for the territory consisting of a popularly elected [Legislative Assembly](/wiki/Australian_Capital_Territory_Legislative_Assembly "Australian Capital Territory Legislative Assembly") which elects a [Chief Minister](/wiki/Chief_Minister_of_the_Australian_Capital_Territory "Chief Minister of the Australian Capital Territory") from among its membership who, in turn, appoints an Executive consisting of a number of Ministers.

The executive power of the territory rests with the [ACT government](/wiki/Government_of_the_Australian_Capital_Territory "Government of the Australian Capital Territory"), headed by the Chief Minister (currently the [Labor Party](/wiki/Australian_Labor_Party_(Australian_Capital_Territory_Branch) "Australian Labor Party (Australian Capital Territory Branch)")'s [Andrew Barr](/wiki/Andrew_Barr "Andrew Barr")) and consists of ministers appointed by them. The ministers are supported by the ACT Public Service, which is arranged into directorates, and a number of public authorities.[[63]](#cite_note-67) The Chief Minister is the equivalent of a [State Premier](/wiki/Premiers_and_chief_ministers_of_the_Australian_states_and_territories "Premiers and chief ministers of the Australian states and territories") and sits on the National Cabinet.[[64]](#cite_note-68) Unlike the states, there is no [vice-regal representative](/wiki/Governors_of_the_Australian_states "Governors of the Australian states") who chairs the executive government council. The Chief Minister performs many of the roles that a state governor normally holds in the context of a state; however, the Speaker of the Legislative Assembly gazettes the laws and summons meetings of the Assembly.

The [ACT Labor party](/wiki/Australian_Labor_Party_(Australian_Capital_Territory_Branch) "Australian Labor Party (Australian Capital Territory Branch)") has been in power since 2001.

The legislative power of the territory is vested in the unicameral [Legislative Assembly](/wiki/Australian_Capital_Territory_Legislative_Assembly "Australian Capital Territory Legislative Assembly"). The assembly has 25 [members](/wiki/Template:ACTCurrentMLAs "Template:ACTCurrentMLAs"), elected from [five electorates](/wiki/Electorates_of_the_Australian_Capital_Territory "Electorates of the Australian Capital Territory") using the [Hare-Clark](/wiki/Hare%E2%80%93Clark_electoral_system "Hare–Clark electoral system") single transferable voting system.[[65]](#cite_note-69) The assembly is presided over by the Speaker (currently the Liberal Party's [Jeremy Hanson](/wiki/Jeremy_Hanson "Jeremy Hanson")). The Assembly has almost all of the same powers as the state parliaments, the power to "make laws for the peace, order, and good government of the Territory", with limited exceptions relating to the territory's unique relationship with the commonwealth.[[66]](#cite_note-70) The Hare-Clark voting system was adopted after [a referendum in 1992](/wiki/1992_Australian_Capital_Territory_electoral_system_referendum "1992 Australian Capital Territory electoral system referendum") and was entrenched by another referendum in 1995.[[67]](#cite_note-71) The electoral system cannot be changed except by a two-thirds majority in the Assembly or a majority vote of support at a public referendum.[[68]](#cite_note-72)

There is no level of [local government](/wiki/Local_government_in_Australia "Local government in Australia") below the territory government as in the states and the functions associated with local government are carried out principally by the [Transport Canberra and City Services Directorate](/wiki/Transport_Canberra_%26_City_Services "Transport Canberra & City Services").[[69]](#cite_note-73) There is an indigenous voice to the ACT government, called the [Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Elected Body](/wiki/Australian_Indigenous_advisory_bodies#Australian_Capital_Territory "Australian Indigenous advisory bodies").[[70]](#cite_note-74)

Despite the wide powers of the territory government, the federal government continues to have power over the territory. This includes an unused power to dissolve the assembly and appoint a caretaker government in extraordinary circumstances.[[71]](#cite_note-75) The federal and territory governments share some officers, such as the [Ombudsman](/wiki/Commonwealth_Ombudsman "Commonwealth Ombudsman").[[72]](#cite_note-76) The federal parliament also retains the power to make any law for the territory under [section 122 of the Constitution](/wiki/Section_122_of_the_Constitution_of_Australia "Section 122 of the Constitution of Australia") and an exclusive power to legislate for the "seat of government".[[61]](#cite_note-:10-65)[[73]](#cite_note-77) Territory laws which conflict with federal law are inoperable to the extent of the inconsistency.[[74]](#cite_note-78)[[75]](#cite_note-79) Land in the territory that is designated to be "National Land" under federal law remains under the control of the federal government, usually represented by the [National Capital Authority](/wiki/National_Capital_Authority "National Capital Authority").[[76]](#cite_note-80) The federal parliament can disallow laws enacted by the Assembly by a joint resolution of both houses of Parliament, a power which replaced a federal executive veto in 2011.[[77]](#cite_note-81)[[78]](#cite_note-vetopower-82)

### Judiciary and policing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Australian_Capital_Territory&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Judiciary and policing")]

See also: [Crime in the Australian Capital Territory](/wiki/Crime_in_the_Australian_Capital_Territory "Crime in the Australian Capital Territory")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/New_Law_Courts_of_the_Australian_Capital_Territory.jpg/250px-New_Law_Courts_of_the_Australian_Capital_Territory.jpg)](/wiki/File:New_Law_Courts_of_the_Australian_Capital_Territory.jpg)

The Law Courts in Civic houses the Supreme Court and the Magistrates' Court

The judicial power of the territory is exercised by the territory courts. These are the [Supreme Court of the Australian Capital Territory](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_Australian_Capital_Territory "Supreme Court of the Australian Capital Territory"), the [Magistrates Court of the Australian Capital Territory](/wiki/Magistrates_Court_of_the_Australian_Capital_Territory "Magistrates Court of the Australian Capital Territory") (with summary jurisdiction), and the [ACT Civil and Administrative Tribunal](/wiki/ACT_Civil_and_Administrative_Tribunal "ACT Civil and Administrative Tribunal"). From 2001, appeals from the Supreme Court are heard by a panel of Supreme Court judges sitting as the Court of Appeal.[[79]](#cite_note-83) The current Chief Justice is [Lucy McCallum](/wiki/Lucy_McCallum "Lucy McCallum") and the current Chief Magistrate is [Lorraine Walker](/wiki/Lorraine_Walker "Lorraine Walker").

The [Federal Court](/wiki/Federal_Court_of_Australia "Federal Court of Australia") has concurrent jurisdiction over civil matters arising under territory law, a fact which has become increasingly important to the practice of [defamation law](/wiki/Defamation "Defamation") across Australia.[[80]](#cite_note-84)[[81]](#cite_note-85)[[82]](#cite_note-86)

Policing services are provided by the [ACT Policing](/wiki/ACT_Policing "ACT Policing") unit of the [Australian Federal Police](/wiki/Australian_Federal_Police "Australian Federal Police") under agreements between the territory government, the federal government, and the police force.[[83]](#cite_note-87)[[84]](#cite_note-88)[[85]](#cite_note-89) [Canberra](/wiki/Canberra "Canberra") had the lowest rate of crime of any capital city in Australia as of February 2019[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Australian_Capital_Territory&action=edit).[[86]](#cite_note-90)

### Federal representation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Australian_Capital_Territory&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Federal representation")]

In the Australian [federal parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_Australia "Parliament of Australia"), the territory is represented by three members of the [House of Representatives](/wiki/Australian_House_of_Representatives "Australian House of Representatives"), for the [Division of Bean](/wiki/Division_of_Bean "Division of Bean"), the [Division of Canberra](/wiki/Division_of_Canberra "Division of Canberra") and the [Division of Fenner](/wiki/Division_of_Fenner "Division of Fenner") and two [Senators](/wiki/Australian_Senate "Australian Senate"). The member for Bean also represents voters of [Norfolk Island](/wiki/Norfolk_Island "Norfolk Island") while the member for Fenner also represent voters of the [Jervis Bay Territory](/wiki/Jervis_Bay_Territory "Jervis Bay Territory"). Senators representing the ACT also represent voters of Norfolk Island and the Jervis Bay Territory.

### Jervis Bay Territory

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Australian_Capital_Territory&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Jervis Bay Territory")]

Main article: [Jervis Bay Territory](/wiki/Jervis_Bay_Territory "Jervis Bay Territory")

Jervis Bay Territory, created in 1915, is a separate territory but, by the *[Jervis Bay Territory Acceptance Act 1915](/wiki/Jervis_Bay_Territory_Acceptance_Act_1915 "Jervis Bay Territory Acceptance Act 1915")* it is subject to ACT law.[[87]](#cite_note-Spagnolo2015-91)[[20]](#cite_note-:1-23) ACT magistrates conduct court in Jervis Bay Territory.[[88]](#cite_note-92)

ACT population density by mesh blocks (MB), according to the 2016 census

The [Australian Bureau of Statistics](/wiki/Australian_Bureau_of_Statistics "Australian Bureau of Statistics") estimates that the population of the territory was 453,324 on 31 December 2021, with an annual growth in 2021 of 0.4%.[[13]](#cite_note-ABSPop-16) A 2019 projection estimated the population would reach to approximately 700,000 by 2058.[[89]](#cite_note-93)[[90]](#cite_note-94)

The overwhelming majority of the population reside in the city of Canberra.[[13]](#cite_note-ABSPop-16)

At the [2016 census](/wiki/2016_Australian_census "2016 Australian census"), the median weekly income for people in the territory aged over 15 was $998 while the national average was $662.[[21]](#cite_note-census-24)

The average level of degree qualification in the territory is higher than the national average. Within the territory, 37.1% of the population hold a bachelor's degree level or above education compared to the national figure of 20%.[[21]](#cite_note-census-24)

### City and towns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Australian_Capital_Territory&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: City and towns")]

Main articles: [Canberra](/wiki/Canberra "Canberra") and [Suburbs of Canberra](/wiki/Suburbs_of_Canberra "Suburbs of Canberra")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/BikepathCanberra.jpg/250px-BikepathCanberra.jpg)](/wiki/File:BikepathCanberra.jpg)

Bikepath to Weston Creek.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/Inner-canberra_01MJC.png/250px-Inner-canberra_01MJC.png)](/wiki/File:Inner-canberra_01MJC.png)

Inner Canberra demonstrates some aspects of the Griffin plan, in particular the [Parliamentary Triangle](/wiki/Parliamentary_Triangle,_Canberra "Parliamentary Triangle, Canberra").

Major settlements include the city of [Canberra](/wiki/Canberra "Canberra") and the towns of [Williamsdale](/wiki/Williamsdale,_Australian_Capital_Territory "Williamsdale, Australian Capital Territory"), [Oaks Estate](/wiki/Oaks_Estate,_Australian_Capital_Territory "Oaks Estate, Australian Capital Territory"), [Uriarra](/wiki/Uriarra,_Australian_Capital_Territory "Uriarra, Australian Capital Territory"), [Tharwa](/wiki/Tharwa,_Australian_Capital_Territory "Tharwa, Australian Capital Territory") and [Hall](/wiki/Hall,_Australian_Capital_Territory "Hall, Australian Capital Territory").

The urban areas of the territory are organised into a hierarchy of districts, town centres, group centres, local suburbs as well as other industrial areas and villages. There are seven districts (with an eighth currently under construction), each of which is divided into smaller suburbs, and most of which have a town centre which is the focus of commercial and social activities. The districts were settled in the following chronological order:

* [North Canberra](/wiki/North_Canberra "North Canberra"): mostly settled in the 1920s and '30s, with expansion up to the 1960s, now 14 suburbs;
* [South Canberra](/wiki/South_Canberra "South Canberra"): settled from the 1920s to '60s, 13 suburbs;
* [Woden Valley](/wiki/Woden_Valley "Woden Valley"): first settled in 1963, 12 suburbs;
* [Belconnen](/wiki/Belconnen "Belconnen"): first settled in 1967, 25 suburbs;
* [Weston Creek](/wiki/Weston_Creek "Weston Creek"): settled in 1969, 8 suburbs;
* [Tuggeranong](/wiki/Tuggeranong "Tuggeranong"): settled in 1974, 19 suburbs;
* [Gungahlin](/wiki/Gungahlin "Gungahlin"): settled in the early 1990s, 18 suburbs although only 15 are developed or under development;
* [Molonglo Valley](/wiki/Molonglo_Valley "Molonglo Valley"): first suburbs currently under construction.

The North and South Canberra districts are substantially based on Walter Burley Griffin's designs.[[91]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWigmore197164-95) In 1967, the then [National Capital Development Commission](/wiki/National_Capital_Development_Commission "National Capital Development Commission") adopted the "Y Plan" which laid out future urban development in the territory a series of central shopping and commercial area known as the 'town centres' linked by freeways, the layout of which roughly resembled the shape of the letter Y,[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESparke1988154–155-96) with Tuggeranong at the base of the Y and Belconnen and Gungahlin located at the ends of the arms of the Y.[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESparke1988154–155-96)

The [2021 census](/wiki/2021_Australian_census "2021 Australian census") showed that 32.5% of territory inhabitants were [born overseas](/wiki/Immigration_to_Australia "Immigration to Australia").[[94]](#cite_note-2021_Census-100) Of inhabitants born outside of Australia, the most prevalent countries of birth were India, England, China, Nepal and New Zealand.[[94]](#cite_note-2021_Census-100)

2.0% of the population, or 8,949 people, identified as [Indigenous Australians](/wiki/Indigenous_Australians "Indigenous Australians") ([Aboriginal Australians](/wiki/Aboriginal_Australians "Aboriginal Australians") and [Torres Strait Islanders](/wiki/Torres_Strait_Islanders "Torres Strait Islanders")) in 2021.[[note 6]](#cite_note-104)[[94]](#cite_note-2021_Census-100)

### Language

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Australian_Capital_Territory&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Language")]

The other languages most commonly spoken at home were [Mandarin](/wiki/Standard_Mandarin "Standard Mandarin") (3.2%), [Nepali](/wiki/Nepali_language "Nepali language") (1.3%), [Vietnamese](/wiki/Vietnamese_language "Vietnamese language") (1.1%), [Punjabi](/wiki/Punjabi_language "Punjabi language") (1.1%), [Hindi](/wiki/Hindi "Hindi") (1.0%).[[94]](#cite_note-2021_Census-100)
