[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Australian_Capital_Territory&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Religion")]

The most common responses in the [2021 census](/wiki/2021_Australian_census "2021 Australian census") for religion in the territory were No Religion (43.5%), Catholic (19.3%), Anglican (8.2%), Not stated (5.2%) and Hinduism (4.5%).[[94]](#cite_note-2021_Census-100)

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Australian_Capital_Territory&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Culture")]

### Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Australian_Capital_Territory&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Education")]

Main article: [Education in the Australian Capital Territory](/wiki/Education_in_the_Australian_Capital_Territory "Education in the Australian Capital Territory")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/John_Curtin_School_of_Medical_Research_in_Canberra.jpg/250px-John_Curtin_School_of_Medical_Research_in_Canberra.jpg)](/wiki/File:John_Curtin_School_of_Medical_Research_in_Canberra.jpg)

The John Curtin School of Medical Research, [Australian National University](/wiki/Australian_National_University "Australian National University")

Almost all educational institutions in the territory are located within [Canberra](/wiki/Canberra "Canberra"). The territory government ("public" sector) school system is mostly divided into [Pre-School](/wiki/Pre-School "Pre-School"), Primary School (K-6), High School (7–10) and [College](/wiki/College#Australia "College") (11–12). Many private sector high schools include years 11 and 12 and are referred to as colleges. Children are required to attend school until they turn 17 under the territory government's "Learn or Earn" policy.[[96]](#cite_note-105)

In February 2005 there were 140 [public and non-governmental schools](/wiki/List_of_schools_in_the_Australian_Capital_Territory "List of schools in the Australian Capital Territory") in the territory; 96 operated by the government and 44 independently.[[97]](#cite_note-106) In 2025, there were 83,259 students in the territory school system. 60.2% of the students were enrolled in government schools with the remaining 39.8% in non-government schools. There were 46,518 students in primary school, 24,816 in high school and 11,925 in college.[[98]](#cite_note-107)

As of May 2004, 30% of people in the territory aged 15–64 had a level of educational attainment equal to at least a bachelor's degree, significantly higher than the national average of 19%.[[99]](#cite_note-108) The two main tertiary institutions are the [Australian National University](/wiki/Australian_National_University "Australian National University") (ANU) in [Acton](/wiki/Acton,_Australian_Capital_Territory "Acton, Australian Capital Territory") and the [University of Canberra](/wiki/University_of_Canberra "University of Canberra") (UC) in [Bruce](/wiki/Bruce,_Australian_Capital_Territory "Bruce, Australian Capital Territory"). There are also two religious university campuses in Canberra: Signadou is a campus of the [Australian Catholic University](/wiki/Australian_Catholic_University "Australian Catholic University") and St Mark's Theological College is a campus of [Charles Sturt University](/wiki/Charles_Sturt_University "Charles Sturt University"). Tertiary level vocational education is also available through the multi-campus [Canberra Institute of Technology](/wiki/Canberra_Institute_of_Technology "Canberra Institute of Technology") (CIT).

The [Australian Defence Force Academy](/wiki/Australian_Defence_Force_Academy "Australian Defence Force Academy") (ADFA) and the [Royal Military College, Duntroon](/wiki/Royal_Military_College,_Duntroon "Royal Military College, Duntroon") (RMC) are in the suburb of [Campbell](/wiki/Campbell,_Australian_Capital_Territory "Campbell, Australian Capital Territory") in Canberra's inner northeast. ADFA teaches military undergraduates and [postgraduates](/wiki/Quaternary_education "Quaternary education") and is officially a campus of the [University of New South Wales](/wiki/University_of_New_South_Wales "University of New South Wales") while Duntroon provides [Australian Army](/wiki/Australian_Army "Australian Army") [Officer](/wiki/Officer_(armed_forces) "Officer (armed forces)") [training](/wiki/Training "Training").

The [Academy of Interactive Entertainment](/wiki/Academy_of_Interactive_Entertainment "Academy of Interactive Entertainment") (AIE) offers courses in computer game development and 3D animation.

### Sport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Australian_Capital_Territory&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Sport")]

Main article: [Sport in the Australian Capital Territory](/wiki/Sport_in_the_Australian_Capital_Territory "Sport in the Australian Capital Territory")

The territory is home to a number of major professional sports league franchise teams including the [ACT Brumbies](/wiki/Brumbies "Brumbies") (Rugby Union), [Canberra United](/wiki/Canberra_United "Canberra United") (Soccer), [Canberra Raiders](/wiki/Canberra_Raiders "Canberra Raiders") (Rugby League), [Canberra Capitals](/wiki/Canberra_Capitals "Canberra Capitals") (Basketball) and the [Canberra Chill](/wiki/Canberra_Chill "Canberra Chill")[[100]](#cite_note-109)[[101]](#cite_note-110) (Field Hockey).
[Big Bash League](/wiki/Big_Bash_League "Big Bash League") matches and other international [cricket](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket") matches are held at the [Manuka Oval](/wiki/Manuka_Oval "Manuka Oval"). The [ACT Comets](/wiki/ACT_Comets "ACT Comets") (also known as the Canberra Comets) are a cricket team in the territory. The Comets are the premier team of Cricket ACT which is affiliated with [Cricket Australia](/wiki/Cricket_Australia "Cricket Australia").

The [Prime Minister's XI](/wiki/Prime_Minister%27s_XI "Prime Minister's XI") (Cricket), started by [Robert Menzies](/wiki/Robert_Menzies "Robert Menzies") in the 1950s and revived by [Bob Hawke](/wiki/Bob_Hawke "Bob Hawke") in 1984, has been played every year at [Manuka Oval](/wiki/Manuka_Oval "Manuka Oval") against an overseas touring team.

The [Greater Western Sydney Giants](/wiki/Greater_Western_Sydney_Giants "Greater Western Sydney Giants") (Football) play three regular season matches a year and one pre-season match in Canberra at Manuka Oval.

### Arts and entertainment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Australian_Capital_Territory&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Arts and entertainment")]

See also: [Music of Canberra](/wiki/Music_of_Canberra "Music of Canberra")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/NatMusAus_Main_Entrance_Strip.jpg/250px-NatMusAus_Main_Entrance_Strip.jpg)](/wiki/File:NatMusAus_Main_Entrance_Strip.jpg)

The [National Museum of Australia](/wiki/National_Museum_of_Australia "National Museum of Australia") established in 2001 records Australia's social history and is one of the territory's more architecturally daring buildings.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/War_memorial_01.jpg/250px-War_memorial_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:War_memorial_01.jpg)

The [Australian War Memorial](/wiki/Australian_War_Memorial "Australian War Memorial")

The territory is home to many national monuments and institutions such as the [Australian War Memorial](/wiki/Australian_War_Memorial "Australian War Memorial"), the [National Gallery of Australia](/wiki/National_Gallery_of_Australia "National Gallery of Australia"), the [National Portrait Gallery](/wiki/National_Portrait_Gallery_(Australia) "National Portrait Gallery (Australia)"), the [National Library](/wiki/National_Library_of_Australia "National Library of Australia"),[[102]](#cite_note-map-111) the [National Archives](/wiki/National_Archives_of_Australia "National Archives of Australia"),[[103]](#cite_note-112) the [Australian Academy of Science](/wiki/Australian_Academy_of_Science "Australian Academy of Science"),[[104]](#cite_note-113) the [National Film and Sound Archive](/wiki/National_Film_and_Sound_Archive "National Film and Sound Archive") and the [National Museum](/wiki/National_Museum_of_Australia "National Museum of Australia").[[102]](#cite_note-map-111) Many Commonwealth government buildings in the territory are open to the public, including [Parliament House](/wiki/Parliament_House,_Canberra "Parliament House, Canberra"), the [High Court](/wiki/High_Court_of_Australia "High Court of Australia") and the [Royal Australian Mint](/wiki/Royal_Australian_Mint "Royal Australian Mint").[[105]](#cite_note-114)[[106]](#cite_note-115)[[107]](#cite_note-116)

Lake Burley Griffin is the site of the [Captain James Cook Memorial](/wiki/Captain_James_Cook_Memorial "Captain James Cook Memorial") and the [National Carillon](/wiki/National_Carillon "National Carillon").[[102]](#cite_note-map-111) Other sites of interest include the [Telstra Tower](/wiki/Telstra_Tower "Telstra Tower"), the [Australian National Botanic Gardens](/wiki/Australian_National_Botanic_Gardens "Australian National Botanic Gardens"), the [National Zoo and Aquarium](/wiki/National_Zoo_and_Aquarium "National Zoo and Aquarium"), the [National Dinosaur Museum](/wiki/National_Dinosaur_Museum "National Dinosaur Museum") and [Questacon – the National Science and Technology Centre](/wiki/National_Science_and_Technology_Centre,_Canberra "National Science and Technology Centre, Canberra").[[102]](#cite_note-map-111)[[108]](#cite_note-117)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/NLA_Canberra-01JAC.JPG/250px-NLA_Canberra-01JAC.JPG)](/wiki/File:NLA_Canberra-01JAC.JPG)

A copy of every book published in Australia is required by law to be held by the [National Library of Australia](/wiki/National_Library_of_Australia "National Library of Australia").[[109]](#cite_note-118)

The [Canberra Museum and Gallery](/wiki/Canberra_Museum_and_Gallery "Canberra Museum and Gallery") in [the city](/wiki/City,_Australian_Capital_Territory "City, Australian Capital Territory") is a repository of local history and art, housing a permanent collection and visiting exhibitions.[[110]](#cite_note-119) Several historic homes are open to the public: Lanyon and Tuggeranong Homesteads in the [Tuggeranong Valley](/wiki/Tuggeranong "Tuggeranong"),[[111]](#cite_note-120)[[112]](#cite_note-121) Mugga-Mugga in [Symonston](/wiki/Symonston,_Australian_Capital_Territory "Symonston, Australian Capital Territory"),[[113]](#cite_note-122) and Blundells' Cottage in [Parkes](/wiki/Parkes,_Australian_Capital_Territory "Parkes, Australian Capital Territory") all display the lifestyle of the early European settlers.[[114]](#cite_note-BLUNDELL-123) Calthorpes' House in [Red Hill](/wiki/Red_Hill,_Australian_Capital_Territory "Red Hill, Australian Capital Territory") is a well-preserved example of a 1920s house from Canberra's very early days.[[115]](#cite_note-124)

The territory has many venues for live music and theatre: the [Canberra Theatre and Playhouse](/wiki/Canberra_Theatre "Canberra Theatre") which hosts many major concerts and productions;[[116]](#cite_note-125) and [Llewellyn Hall](/wiki/Llewellyn_Hall "Llewellyn Hall") (within the [ANU School of Music](/wiki/ANU_School_of_Music "ANU School of Music")), a world-class concert hall are two of the most notable.[[117]](#cite_note-rg-126) [The Albert Hall](/wiki/Albert_Hall,_Canberra "Albert Hall, Canberra") was Canberra's first performing arts venue, opened in 1928. It was the original performance venue for theatre groups such as the Canberra Repertory Society.[[118]](#cite_note-127)

There are numerous bars and nightclubs which also offer live entertainment, particularly concentrated in the areas of [Dickson](/wiki/Dickson,_Australian_Capital_Territory "Dickson, Australian Capital Territory"), [Kingston](/wiki/Kingston,_Australian_Capital_Territory "Kingston, Australian Capital Territory") and [the city](/wiki/City,_Australian_Capital_Territory "City, Australian Capital Territory").[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVaisutis2009283–285-128) Most town centres have facilities for a community theatre and a cinema, and all have a library.[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUniversal_Publishers200710–12-129) Popular cultural events include the [National Folk Festival](/wiki/National_Folk_Festival_(Australia) "National Folk Festival (Australia)"), the [Royal Canberra Show](/wiki/Royal_Canberra_Show "Royal Canberra Show"), the [Summernats](/wiki/Summernats "Summernats") car festival, [Enlighten](/wiki/Enlighten_Canberra "Enlighten Canberra") festival and the [National Multicultural Festival](/wiki/National_Multicultural_Festival "National Multicultural Festival") in February.[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVaisutis2009278-130)

### Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Australian_Capital_Territory&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Media")]

The territory has a daily newspaper, *[The Canberra Times](/wiki/The_Canberra_Times "The Canberra Times")*, which was established in 1926.[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWigmore197187-131)[[123]](#cite_note-132) There are also several free weekly publications, including news magazines *City News* and *Canberra Weekly.*

Major Australian metropolitan newspapers such as the *[Daily Telegraph](/wiki/Daily_Telegraph_(Sydney) "Daily Telegraph (Sydney)")* and *[Sydney Morning Herald](/wiki/Sydney_Morning_Herald "Sydney Morning Herald")* from Sydney, *[The Age](/wiki/The_Age "The Age")* and *[Herald Sun](/wiki/Herald_Sun "Herald Sun")* from Melbourne as well as national publications *[The Australian](/wiki/The_Australian "The Australian")* and the *[Australian Financial Review](/wiki/Australian_Financial_Review "Australian Financial Review")* are also available.

There are a number of AM and FM stations broadcasting throughout the territory ([AM/FM Listing](/wiki/List_of_radio_station_callsigns_in_the_Australian_Capital_Territory "List of radio station callsigns in the Australian Capital Territory")). The main commercial operators are the [Capital Radio Network](/wiki/Capital_Radio_Network "Capital Radio Network") ([2CA](/wiki/2CA "2CA") and [2CC](/wiki/2CC "2CC")), and [Southern Cross Austereo](/wiki/Southern_Cross_Austereo "Southern Cross Austereo")/[ARN Media](/wiki/ARN_Media "ARN Media") ([Hit 104.7](/wiki/Hit_104.7 "Hit 104.7") and [Mix 106.3](/wiki/Mix_106.3 "Mix 106.3")). There are also several community operated stations as well as the local and national stations of the [Australian Broadcasting Corporation](/wiki/Australian_Broadcasting_Corporation "Australian Broadcasting Corporation").

A DAB+ digital radio trial is also in operation, it simulcasts some of the AM/FM stations, and also provides several digital only stations ([DAB+ Trial Listing](/wiki/Digital_radio_in_Australia#Canberra "Digital radio in Australia")).

Five free-to-air television stations service the territory:

* [ABC](/wiki/ABC_Television_(Australian_TV_network) "ABC Television (Australian TV network)") Canberra ([ABC](/wiki/ABC_Canberra_(TV_station) "ABC Canberra (TV station)"))
* [SBS](/wiki/Special_Broadcasting_Service#Television "Special Broadcasting Service") New South Wales/Australian Capital Territory ([SBS](/wiki/SBS_(Australian_TV_channel) "SBS (Australian TV channel)"))
* [WIN Television](/wiki/WIN_Television "WIN Television") Southern NSW & ACT ([WIN](/wiki/WIN_(TV_station) "WIN (TV station)")) – [Nine Network](/wiki/Nine_Network "Nine Network") affiliate
* [Prime7](/wiki/Prime7 "Prime7") Southern NSW & ACT ([CBN](/wiki/CBN_(Australian_TV_station) "CBN (Australian TV station)")) – A [Seven Network](/wiki/Seven_Network "Seven Network") owned & operated station, [Seven Network](/wiki/Seven_Network "Seven Network") affiliate
* [Southern Cross 10](/wiki/10_(Southern_Cross_Austereo) "10 (Southern Cross Austereo)") Southern NSW & ACT ([CTC](/wiki/CTC_(TV_station) "CTC (TV station)")) – [Network 10](/wiki/Network_10 "Network 10") affiliate

Each station broadcasts a primary channel and several [multichannels](/wiki/Digital_terrestrial_television_in_Australia#Additional_channels "Digital terrestrial television in Australia").

[Pay television](/wiki/Pay_television "Pay television") services are available from [Foxtel](/wiki/Foxtel "Foxtel") (via satellite) and telecommunications company [TransACT](/wiki/TransACT "TransACT") (via cable).[[124]](#cite_note-133)

## Infrastructure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Australian_Capital_Territory&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Infrastructure")]

### Health

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Australian_Capital_Territory&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Health")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/Canberra_Hospital.jpg/250px-Canberra_Hospital.jpg)](/wiki/File:Canberra_Hospital.jpg)

The [Canberra Hospital](/wiki/Canberra_Hospital "Canberra Hospital").

The territory has two large public hospitals both located in Canberra: the approximately 600-bed [Canberra Hospital](/wiki/Canberra_Hospital "Canberra Hospital") in [Garran](/wiki/Garran,_Australian_Capital_Territory "Garran, Australian Capital Territory") and the 174-bed Calvary Public Hospital in [Bruce](/wiki/Bruce,_Australian_Capital_Territory "Bruce, Australian Capital Territory"). Both are teaching institutions.[[125]](#cite_note-hos-134)[[126]](#cite_note-135)[[127]](#cite_note-calvary-act1-136)[[128]](#cite_note-137) The largest private hospital is the Calvary John James Hospital in [Deakin](/wiki/Deakin,_Australian_Capital_Territory "Deakin, Australian Capital Territory").[[129]](#cite_note-138)[[130]](#cite_note-139) Calvary Private Hospital in Bruce and [Healthscope](/wiki/Healthscope "Healthscope")'s National Capital Private Hospital in Garran are also major healthcare providers.[[125]](#cite_note-hos-134)[[127]](#cite_note-calvary-act1-136)

The territory has 10 aged care facilities. Territory hospitals receive emergency cases from throughout southern New South Wales,[[131]](#cite_note-140) and [ACT Ambulance Service](/wiki/ACT_Ambulance_Service "ACT Ambulance Service") is one of four operational agencies of the [ACT Emergency Services Authority](/wiki/ACT_Emergency_Services_Authority "ACT Emergency Services Authority").[[132]](#cite_note-141) [NETS](/wiki/Newborn_Emergency_Transport_Service "Newborn Emergency Transport Service") provides a dedicated ambulance service for inter-hospital transport of sick newborns within the ACT and into surrounding New South Wales.[[133]](#cite_note-142)

### Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Australian_Capital_Territory&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Transport")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f0/Tuggeranong_Parkway.jpg/250px-Tuggeranong_Parkway.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tuggeranong_Parkway.jpg)

Aerial view of Tuggeranong Parkway, a major highway which links Canberra's city centre with [Tuggeranong](/wiki/Tuggeranong "Tuggeranong").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/New_terminal_building_at_Canberra_Airport_cropped2.jpg/250px-New_terminal_building_at_Canberra_Airport_cropped2.jpg)](/wiki/File:New_terminal_building_at_Canberra_Airport_cropped2.jpg)

[Canberra Airport](/wiki/Canberra_Airport "Canberra Airport") terminal

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Canberra_Railway_Station.jpg/250px-Canberra_Railway_Station.jpg)](/wiki/File:Canberra_Railway_Station.jpg)

[Canberra railway station](/wiki/Canberra_railway_station "Canberra railway station")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/BUS877-04.jpg/250px-BUS877-04.jpg)](/wiki/File:BUS877-04.jpg)

[ACTION](/wiki/ACTION "ACTION") [Ansair](/wiki/Ansair "Ansair") bodied [Renault PR100](/wiki/Renault_PR100 "Renault PR100").2

The automobile is by far the dominant form of transport in the territory.[[134]](#cite_note-road-143) The city is laid out so that arterial roads connecting inhabited clusters run through undeveloped areas of open land or forest, which results in a low population density;[[135]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPenguin_Books_Australia200023–25-144) this also means that idle land is available for the development of future transport corridors if necessary without the need to build tunnels or acquire developed residential land. In contrast, other capital cities in Australia have substantially less green space.[[136]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPenguin_Books_Australia20003–6,_32–35,_53–59,_74–77,_90–91,_101–104-145)

[Suburbs of Canberra](/wiki/Suburbs_of_Canberra "Suburbs of Canberra") are generally connected by [parkways](/wiki/Parkway "Parkway")—limited access [dual carriageway](/wiki/Dual_carriageway "Dual carriageway") roads[[134]](#cite_note-road-143)[[137]](#cite_note-146) with speed limits generally set at a maximum of 100 km/h (62 mph).[[138]](#cite_note-147)[[139]](#cite_note-148) An example is the [Tuggeranong Parkway](/wiki/Tuggeranong_Parkway "Tuggeranong Parkway") which links Canberra's CBD and Tuggeranong, and bypasses Weston Creek.[[140]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUniversal_Publishers200757,_67,_77-149) In most districts, discrete residential suburbs are bounded by main arterial roads with only a few residential linking in, to deter non-local traffic from cutting through areas of housing.[[141]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUniversal_Publishers20071–100-150)

[ACTION](/wiki/ACTION "ACTION"), the government-operated bus service, provides public transport route services in the territory.[[142]](#cite_note-151) [CDC Canberra](/wiki/CDC_Canberra "CDC Canberra") provides bus services between the territory and nearby areas of New South Wales ([Murrumbateman](/wiki/Murrumbateman "Murrumbateman") and [Yass](/wiki/Yass,_New_South_Wales "Yass, New South Wales"))[[143]](#cite_note-152) and as Qcity Transit ([Queanbeyan](/wiki/Queanbeyan "Queanbeyan")).[[144]](#cite_note-153) A [light rail line](/wiki/Light_rail_in_Canberra "Light rail in Canberra") that opened in April 2019 links the CBD with the northern district of [Gungahlin](/wiki/Gungahlin "Gungahlin"). At the 2016 census, 7.1% of the journeys to work involved public transport while 4.5% were on foot.[[145]](#cite_note-Census2016-154)

There are two local taxi companies. [Aerial Capital Group](/wiki/Aerial_Consolidated_Transport "Aerial Consolidated Transport") enjoyed monopoly status until the arrival of Cabxpress in 2007.[[146]](#cite_note-155) In October 2015, the territory government passed legislation to regulate ride sharing, allowing ride share services including [Uber](/wiki/Uber "Uber") to operate legally in the territory.[[147]](#cite_note-156)[[148]](#cite_note-157)[[149]](#cite_note-158) The territory government was the first jurisdiction in Australia to enact legislation to regulate the service.[[150]](#cite_note-159)

An interstate [NSW TrainLink](/wiki/NSW_TrainLink "NSW TrainLink") railway service connects Canberra to Sydney.[[151]](#cite_note-traindestinations-160) [Canberra's railway station](/wiki/Canberra_railway_station "Canberra railway station") is in the inner south suburb of [Kingston](/wiki/Kingston,_Australian_Capital_Territory "Kingston, Australian Capital Territory").[[152]](#cite_note-161) Train services to Melbourne are provided by way of a NSW TrainLink bus service which connects with a rail service between Sydney and Melbourne in Yass, about a one-hour drive from Canberra.[[151]](#cite_note-traindestinations-160)[[153]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPenguin_Books_Australia200020-162)

Canberra is about three hours by road from Sydney on the [Federal Highway](/wiki/Federal_Highway,_Australia "Federal Highway, Australia") (National Highway 23),[[154]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPenguin_Books_Australia2000inside_cover-163) which connects with the [Hume Highway](/wiki/Hume_Highway "Hume Highway") (National Highway 31) near [Goulburn](/wiki/Goulburn "Goulburn"), and seven hours by road from Melbourne on the [Barton Highway](/wiki/Barton_Highway "Barton Highway") (National Highway 25), which joins the Hume Highway at Yass.[[154]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPenguin_Books_Australia2000inside_cover-163) It is a two-hour drive on the [Monaro Highway](/wiki/Monaro_Highway "Monaro Highway") (National Highway 23) to the ski fields of the [Snowy Mountains](/wiki/Snowy_Mountains "Snowy Mountains") and the [Kosciuszko National Park](/wiki/Kosciuszko_National_Park "Kosciuszko National Park").[[153]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPenguin_Books_Australia200020-162) [Batemans Bay](/wiki/Batemans_Bay "Batemans Bay"), a popular holiday spot on the New South Wales coast, is also two hours away via the [Kings Highway](/wiki/Kings_Highway,_Australia "Kings Highway, Australia").[[153]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPenguin_Books_Australia200020-162)

[Canberra Airport](/wiki/Canberra_Airport "Canberra Airport") provides direct domestic services to [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney"), [Melbourne](/wiki/Melbourne "Melbourne"), [Newcastle](/wiki/Newcastle,_New_South_Wales "Newcastle, New South Wales"), [Brisbane](/wiki/Brisbane "Brisbane"), [Adelaide](/wiki/Adelaide "Adelaide"), [Gold Coast](/wiki/Gold_Coast,_Queensland "Gold Coast, Queensland"), [Hobart](/wiki/Hobart "Hobart") and [Perth](/wiki/Perth "Perth"), with connections to other domestic centres.[[155]](#cite_note-164) Regular direct international flights operate to [Nadi](/wiki/Nadi "Nadi") in Fiji.[[156]](#cite_note-165) Canberra Airport is, as of September 2013, designated by the [Australian Government Department of Infrastructure and Regional Development](/wiki/Department_of_Infrastructure_and_Regional_Development "Department of Infrastructure and Regional Development") as a restricted use designated international airport.[[157]](#cite_note-166) Until 2003, the civilian airport shared runways with [RAAF Base Fairbairn](/wiki/Fairbairn_airbase "Fairbairn airbase"). In June of that year, the Air Force base was decommissioned and from that time the airport was fully under civilian control.[[158]](#cite_note-167)

### Utilities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Australian_Capital_Territory&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Utilities")]

See also: [Energy in the Australian Capital Territory](/wiki/Energy_in_the_Australian_Capital_Territory "Energy in the Australian Capital Territory")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/Mount_Majura_solar_farm_and_Majura_Parkway.jpg/250px-Mount_Majura_solar_farm_and_Majura_Parkway.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mount_Majura_solar_farm_and_Majura_Parkway.jpg)

The [Mount Majura Solar Farm](/wiki/Mount_Majura_Solar_Farm "Mount Majura Solar Farm") has a rated output of 2.3 megawatts and was opened on 6 October 2016.[[159]](#cite_note-Solar_Choice-168)

The government-owned [Icon Water](/wiki/Icon_Water "Icon Water"), formerly ACTEW, manages the territory's water and sewerage infrastructure.[[160]](#cite_note-169) [ActewAGL](/wiki/ActewAGL "ActewAGL") is a joint venture between Icon and [AGL](/wiki/Australian_Gas_Light_Company "Australian Gas Light Company") and is a retail provider of territory utility services including water, natural gas, electricity, and also some telecommunications services via a subsidiary [TransACT](/wiki/TransACT "TransACT").[[161]](#cite_note-170)

The territory's water reservoirs are the Corin, Bendora and Cotter dams on the [Cotter River](/wiki/Cotter_River "Cotter River") and the Googong Dam on the Queanbeyan River. Although the Googong Dam is located in New South Wales, it is managed by the territory government.[[162]](#cite_note-171) Icon Water owns the territory's two wastewater treatment plants, located at [Fyshwick](/wiki/Fyshwick "Fyshwick") and on the lower reaches of the [Molonglo River](/wiki/Molonglo_River "Molonglo River").[[163]](#cite_note-172)[[164]](#cite_note-173)

[Electricity for the territory](/wiki/Energy_in_the_Australian_Capital_Territory "Energy in the Australian Capital Territory") mainly comes from the national power grid through substations at [Holt](/wiki/Holt,_Australian_Capital_Territory "Holt, Australian Capital Territory") and [Fyshwick](/wiki/Fyshwick "Fyshwick") (via [Queanbeyan](/wiki/Queanbeyan "Queanbeyan")).[[165]](#cite_note-174) Power was first supplied from a thermal plant built in 1913, near the Molonglo River, but this was finally closed in 1957.[[166]](#cite_note-175)[[167]](#cite_note-176) The ACT has four solar farms, which were opened between 2014 and 2017: [Royalla](/wiki/Royalla_Solar_Farm "Royalla Solar Farm") (rated output of 20 megawatts, 2014),[[168]](#cite_note-177) [Mount Majura](/wiki/Mount_Majura_Solar_Farm "Mount Majura Solar Farm") (2.3 MW, 2016),[[159]](#cite_note-Solar_Choice-168) [Mugga Lane](/wiki/Mugga_Lane_Solar_Park "Mugga Lane Solar Park") (13 MW, 2017)[[169]](#cite_note-178) and [Williamsdale](/wiki/Williamsdale_Solar_Farm "Williamsdale Solar Farm") (11 MW, 2017).[[170]](#cite_note-179) In addition numerous houses in Canberra have photovoltaic panels or solar hot water systems. In 2015 and 2016, rooftop solar systems supported by the territory government's feed-in tariff had a capacity of 26.3 megawatts, producing 34,910 MWh. In the same year, retailer-supported schemes had a capacity of 25.2 megawatts and exported 28,815 MWh to the grid (power consumed locally was not recorded).[[171]](#cite_note-180)

The territory has the highest rate with internet access at home (94 per cent of households in 2014–15).[[172]](#cite_note-181)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Australian_Capital_Territory&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Economy")]

Main article: [Economy of the Australian Capital Territory](/wiki/Economy_of_the_Australian_Capital_Territory "Economy of the Australian Capital Territory")

Economic activity in the territory is heavily concentrated around the city of Canberra.

A stable housing market, steady employment and rapid population growth in the 21st century have led to economic prosperity and, in 2011, [CommSec](/wiki/CommSec "CommSec") ranked the territory as the second best performing state or territory in the country.[[173]](#cite_note-182) This trend continued into 2016, when the territory was ranked the third best performing out of all of Australia's states and territories.[[174]](#cite_note-183)

In 2017–18, the territory had the fastest rate of growth in the nation due to a rapid growth in population, a strongly performing higher education sector as well as a significant housing and infrastructure investment.[[175]](#cite_note-:7-184)

Higher education is the territory's largest export industry.[[175]](#cite_note-:7-184)[[176]](#cite_note-:8-185) The territory is home to a significant number of universities and higher education providers. The other major services exports of the territory in 2017–18 were government services and personal travel.[[176]](#cite_note-:8-185) The major goods exports of the territory in 2017–18 were gold coin, legal tender coin, metal structures and fish, though these represent a small proportion of the economy compared to services exports.[[176]](#cite_note-:8-185)

The economy of the territory is largely dependent on the public sector with 30% of the jobs in the territory being in the public sector.[[177]](#cite_note-:9-186) Decisions by the federal government regarding the public service can have a significant impact on the territory's economy.[[177]](#cite_note-:9-186)

The territory's gross state product in 2017–18 was $39.8 billion which represented 2.2% of the gross domestic product of Australia.[[176]](#cite_note-:8-185) In 2017–18 the territory economy grew by 4.0 per cent, the highest growth rate of any jurisdiction in Australia. This brought real economic growth over the three years to June 2018 to 12 per cent.[[178]](#cite_note-187)
